name: Base64 Encoder/Decoder
summary: Encode and decode Base64 text with URL-safe and padding options.
mistakes: Treating encoding as encryption or authentication.
Using the wrong variant (standard vs URL-safe) for the target system.
Decoding in the wrong processing stage and breaking downstream parsing.

faqs.q: Is encoding secure by itself?
faqs.a: No. Encoding changes representation; it does not provide confidentiality or integrity.

faqs.q: When should I prefer URL-safe output?
faqs.a: Use URL-safe output when values are transported in URLs, token segments, or path/query fields.

name: Basic String Operations
summary: Trim, normalize spacing, reverse text, and generate slugs quickly.
mistakes: Applying destructive cleanup without keeping original text.
Running broad replacements before checking match scope.
Ignoring Unicode and locale effects in text normalization.

faqs.q: Are these transformations always reversible?
faqs.a: No. Several cleanup operations are lossy unless the original text is preserved.

faqs.q: Should I run replacements directly on production data?
faqs.a: Prefer staged validation and backups before irreversible transformations.

name: Text Diff Compare Tool
summary: Compare text by words, characters, or lines with visual diff output.
exampleOutput: new status: inactive with highlighted change
mistakes: Using character diff for large multiline files where line mode is clearer.
Reviewing only one side of side-by-side output.
Assuming visual diff output can be applied automatically as a patch.

faqs.q: Which mode should I start with?
faqs.a: Start with line mode for large text, then switch to words/chars for precision.

faqs.q: Can this export a patch file?
faqs.a: No. It is a visual aid; apply changes in your source-control workflow.

name: Hash Generator
summary: Generate MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, and SHA-512 hashes from input text.
mistakes: Using decoded values as trusted data without verification.
Reusing weak secrets or test keys in real environments.
Skipping expiry, audience, issuer, or key trust checks.

faqs.q: Is decode output enough for trust decisions?
faqs.a: No. You must verify signatures and validate required claims/policies.

faqs.q: Can browser helpers replace production security controls?
faqs.a: No. Use audited libraries, key management, and CI validation in production.

name: JSON Formatter/Minifier
summary: Pretty-print or minify JSON while surfacing parse errors clearly.
mistakes: Minifying or transforming data before validating structure.
Assuming XML and JSON conversion is perfectly lossless.
Ignoring schema constraints for downstream systems.

faqs.q: Should I format or minify first?
faqs.a: Format first for review and validation, then minify for transport.

faqs.q: Will XML to JSON always preserve exact structure?
faqs.a: Not always. Data models differ and some context can be lost in conversion.

name: Timestamp Converter
summary: Convert Unix timestamps to ISO datetime and back.
mistakes: Using demo outputs directly in high-security production workflows.
Ignoring timezone/locale assumptions in date conversions.
Storing sensitive samples in shared browser profiles.

faqs.q: Are generated values always suitable for security use?
faqs.a: Validate entropy, policy, and compliance requirements for your environment.

faqs.q: Is browser local storage a backup mechanism?
faqs.a: No. It is convenience storage and can be cleared by browser policy.
